summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the frustration of using Visual Studio 2005's Smart Tags feature for variable renaming, which he considers his most frequent refactoring activity. The default keyboard shortcut (Shift+Alt+F10) is nearly impossible to type comfortably, inherited from Microsoft Office compatibility. He discovers better alternatives like Ctrl+. and his preferred Alt+Down, which intuitively maps to the 'drop down' action. The post concludes with a nuanced take on customization: while learning defaults is generally safer, sometimes poor defaults force you to customize.
key_points: Variable renaming is the most common refactoring activity, making keyboard efficiency crucial
Microsoft's cross-product compatibility (Office and Visual Studio) resulted in an unusable keyboard shortcut
Physical keyboard shortcuts should map intuitively to their conceptual actions (Alt+Down for 'dropping down' a menu)
Excessive customization is self-defeating, but poor defaults sometimes leave no alternative
money_quotes: It's the most psychotic keyboard shortcut ever.
I constantly rename variables. It's probably the single most frequent refactoring activity I do.
Go ahead. Just try to type that. I double dog dare you.
But sometimes the defaults are poorly chosen and you have no alternative but to customize them.
Alt + Down... which is an excellent physical map to the conceptual activity of 'dropping down' a smart tag menu.
key_insight: Good keyboard shortcuts should physically and intuitively map to their conceptual actions, and when defaults fail this test, customization becomes necessary despite its drawbacks.
